> *«This will be the opportunity to illustrate how this infrastructure will integrate into the urban fabric to promote modern and sustainable mobility.»* — Spazio Visibile statement For cross-border workers living in Como or frequently commuting between Lombardy and Ticino, the return of bike sharing opens up new possibilities for sustainable mobility. How will this service integrate with the existing public transport offerings? The goal is to create an efficient intermodal system where bike sharing serves as the **missing link** between the various transport options.

### A sustainable choice for daily cross-border commuters

Cross-border workers who work in Ticino but live in Lombardy may find bike sharing an appealing option for short-distance travel within the city of Como. For example, someone arriving by train at Como Lago station could rent a bike to reach their workplace or an appointment in the city centre, avoiding the use of a private car and helping to reduce traffic and pollution.

Another advantage is flexibility: unlike public transport, which follows fixed schedules, bike sharing allows users to travel according to their own needs. This can be particularly useful for those commuting outside peak hours or who need to make multiple stops during the day.

### Comparing with other Ticino cities: Como takes a step forward

While Ticino already boasts well-established bike sharing services in cities like Lugano and Bellinzona, Como is positioning itself as a new player in the sustainable mobility landscape between Lombardy and Ticino. Como’s service could also attract tourists and commuters travelling between the two regions, offering an economical and eco-friendly alternative to using a car.

However, it remains to be seen how the new system will integrate with other transport options. For instance, it is not yet clear whether agreements will be made with local transport companies to facilitate access to bike sharing through integrated subscriptions. Additionally, the coverage of pick-up points will need to be monitored to ensure sufficient availability, especially in residential and outlying areas.

### Tariffs and subscriptions: what to expect?

Spazio Visibile is developing a pricing system that takes into account the diverse needs of users. Among the options under consideration are:
- Time-based fares: pay only for the duration of use, with reduced rates during less busy times.
- Weekly or monthly subscriptions: discounts for regular users.
- Tourist packages: special rates for short-term visitors to Como.

The final tariffs will be announced at the launch, but the aim is to make the service accessible to everyone, from students and workers to tourists. A possible pricing model could be similar to that used in other Italian cities, where a daily rental costs around €5-10, while a monthly subscription is around €30-50.

### Safety and maintenance: key points to monitor

> Bike sharing systems require constant attention to maintenance and safety to ensure a reliable and secure service. Regular checks on bicycle conditions and docking stations are essential to prevent breakdowns and ensure user safety.

📊 Coverage and accessibility
> The success of Como’s bike sharing service will depend heavily on its coverage. Ensuring a widespread network of pick-up points, especially in residential and peripheral areas, will be crucial to meeting the needs of commuters.

💡 Integration with public transport
> One of the biggest challenges will be seamless integration with existing transport networks. This could involve partnerships with local transport companies to offer combined tickets or discounts for users who combine bike sharing with trains, buses, or ferries.

⚠️ Security measures
> To prevent theft and vandalism, the system should include features such as secure docking stations, real-time tracking of bicycles, and clear user guidelines for proper use and parking.

One of the major issues with the old bike-sharing system was the inadequate maintenance of bicycles and collection points. Spazio Visibile has assured that regular checks will be implemented to ensure user safety. Additionally, collection points will be equipped with monitoring systems to prevent theft and vandalism.

For users, this means greater reliability: fewer chances of finding a bike with flat tires or faulty brakes, and reduced risks linked to outdated infrastructure. However, it will be crucial for the company to maintain a fast-response service to address breakdowns or technical issues promptly.

### What if the service doesn’t take off?

If Como’s bike-sharing system fails to capture users’ interest, there are real risks of another collapse. Possible causes of failure include:
- Tariffs that are too high compared to alternatives like using a car or public transport.
- Insufficient coverage of collection points, especially in outlying areas.
- Frequent malfunctions of bicycles or booking systems.

To avoid these scenarios, Spazio Visibile will need to demonstrate proactive and transparent management, with constant monitoring of user satisfaction and rapid resolution of problems.

📊 Comparative table: How Como’s bike-sharing stacks up against other cities

| City | Number of bikes | Collection points | Indicative tariffs | Year of reopening |
|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Como | 80 | 17 | To be confirmed | 2026 |
| Lugano | 300 | 45 | From CHF 0.50/min | 2018 |
| Bellinzona | 120 | 22 | From CHF 1.00/h | 2020 |

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). [object Object]

To optimize your trips between Como and Ticino, you can use the following tools:
- [Cost calculator for transport](nav:calculator): Compare the costs of bike sharing with other options such as private cars, public transport, or ridesharing services.
- [Interactive map of pickup points](nav:border-map): View the exact location of the 17 stations and plan your journeys in advance.

### Key deadlines to remember

- **30 April 2026**: Official inauguration of bike sharing and service launch.
- By May 2026: Publication of final rates and promotional offers.
- June 2026: Launch of discounted rates for the summer season.

### What if there are no pickup points near your home?

If you live in an area not covered by pickup points, you can report your need through the Spazio Visibile app or website. The company will assess the feasibility of expanding the network based on user requests.

### Conclusions: an opportunity not to be missed

The return of bike sharing in Como represents a real opportunity to improve urban mobility and reduce environmental impact. With 80 refurbished bicycles and 17 strategically located pickup points, the service aims to appeal to both residents and tourists. However, its success will depend on Spazio Visibile’s ability to maintain high service standards and offer competitive rates.

For those who cross the border daily, bike sharing can be a valid alternative for short trips, especially when combined with public transport. All that remains is to wait until 30 April to see how the service evolves and what surprises it has in store.

> 📊 Did you know? Bike sharing can help reduce traffic congestion and lower CO₂ emissions in Como and Ticino.

> 💡 Tip: Use the interactive map to check the nearest pickup point before setting off.

> ⚠️ Important: Always lock your bike securely when not in use to prevent theft.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/como-torna-finalmente-a-pedalare-arrivano-80-nuove-bici-per-il-bike-sharing-dove-trovarle-e-quando/)*

item.title: slowUp Ticino 2026: April 19 for a car-free day
item.description: On Sunday, April 19, 2026, 50 km of roads between Locarno and Bellinzona will be closed to traffic. There will be 18 events across Switzerland, with Ticino hosting the first one featuring live entertainment, farm stops, and activities for all ages.
item.content:encoded:
SlowUp Ticino returns, the annual event that transforms **50 kilometres of roads** between Locarno and Bellinzona into a dedicated artery for pedestrians, cyclists and non-motorized vehicles. The event is scheduled for Sunday, 19 April 2026, from 10:00 to 17:00, along the route passing through the municipalities of Locarno, Muralto, Minusio, Tenero-Contra, Gordola, Cugnasco-Gerra, Cadenazzo, Sant’Antonino, Camorino and Bellinzona.

The initiative, promoted by local organizers and supported by sports and recreational associations, is part of the first edition of the 2026 season of the 18 slowUp events held across Switzerland. The motto chosen for the Ticino edition is clear: ‘Live a car-free day’, with the aim of promoting slow mobility, movement and the discovery of the local area.

### The route and new features
The route, identical to previous editions, will be fully closed to motorized traffic, allowing participants to move freely—respecting the direction of travel—on foot, by bicycle, rollerblades or other non-motorized means. You can choose to cover the entire itinerary or just a section, entering and exiting at any point. The only exception is in Minusio, where the red path will be affected by construction work, causing lane restrictions. Organizers urge participants to pay close attention to avoid congestion and delays, particularly near the construction area.

📊 > Important: Construction may cause temporary delays. Plan your route accordingly and follow signage carefully.

### Entertainment and stops along the route
Along the 50 km of road, participants will find 18 themed stops offering animation, food and entertainment, primarily managed by local associations. Highlights for 2026 include a miniature railway, bouncy castles, farm activities, horseback rides, magic shows, vehicle exhibitions (Firefighters and Rega), tennis, live music, Guggen performances and much more. There will also be ‘farm stops’, with exceptional openings at local farms such as Aerni (Piano di Magadino), Scuderia Resinelli - Progero (Gudo) and Ponzio (Sant’Antonino), where visitors can enjoy tastings and guided tours. For cross-border workers and Ticino residents, slowUp offers a unique chance to experience the region in a different way: free from daily traffic pressures and with the opportunity to uncover hidden gems between Locarno and Bellinzona. The event aligns with a broader push for **sustainable mobility**, which in recent years has seen growing interest in alternatives to private cars—especially in a region like Ticino, where cross-border commuting is widespread.

### Before vs after: what changes for participants
Before slowUp, those who commute daily between Italy and Switzerland plan their journeys around peak hours, carefully navigating queues on the A2 or public transport delays. On Sunday, 19 April 2026, however, the entire stretch between Locarno and Bellinzona becomes a living lab for slow mobility. For those living in Lombardy and working in Ticino, this means stepping away from personal vehicles and exploring alternative routes—perhaps even discovering new ways to access Ticino’s urban centres.

### Practical scenarios: how to plan your visit
Participants have several options to choose from:
- Full route: 50 km from Locarno to Bellinzona, with stops along the way to refresh or eat.
- Partial sections: select just one segment, such as from Gordola to Cadenazzo, to avoid the busiest areas.
- Arrival by train: use the Swiss Federal Railways (SBB CFF FFS) or regional lines to reach Locarno or Bellinzona, then access the slowUp route. Locarno and Bellinzona stations will serve as ideal starting points.

A key consideration is luggage or shopping. Since vehicles—including cars and motorcycles—will be excluded, those needing to transport bulky items should plan ahead, perhaps relying on home delivery services or local shops along the route, which will remain open for the occasion.

### Comparison with similar initiatives
Unlike events like ‘car-free Sundays’ in other Swiss or Italian regions, slowUp Ticino stands out for its continuous route and variety of activities. While other cities limit traffic closures to a few hours or specific zones, here the entire stretch between Locarno and Bellinzona remains motor-vehicle-free for seven consecutive hours, creating an immersive experience in gentle mobility.

### Details to keep in mind: what to bring
Organisers recommend wearing comfortable clothing and bringing water, snacks, and sun protection—especially in open areas. For families with children, inflatable play areas and activities are planned, but it’s advisable to check availability at each stop in advance.

> 📊 Key info
> - Date: Sunday, 19 April 2026
> - Route: Locarno to Bellinzona (50 km)
> - Duration: 7 hours (9:00 AM to 4:00 PM)
> - Access: Free, open to all ages
> - Public transport: Free on SBB CFF FFS trains within Ticino on the day

> ⚠️ Important notes
> - No motor vehicles allowed on the route (including e-bikes and scooters)
> - Bicycles and pedestrians only
> - No parking available along the route—use designated areas outside the closed sections

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). Taking part in slowUp Ticino 2026 is straightforward, but it requires a bit of planning to make the most of the day. Here’s a step-by-step guide to ensure you’re fully prepared.

### 1. Plan your arrival on the route
If you’re coming from outside Canton Ticino, consider taking the train: Locarno and Bellinzona stations are directly connected to Milan, Como, and other Lombard cities. From there, you can access the slowUp route on foot or by bicycle.

> *Practical tip*: Check train schedules in advance, as some services may be adjusted for the event.

### 2. Choose your access point
The route is accessible from any point. If you’re starting from Locarno, you can begin your adventure at Piazza Grande or the Lido. If you prefer Bellinzona, the natural starting point is near the FFS train station.

### 3. Prepare for internal travel
Since cars and motorcycles won’t be allowed, bring only what you can carry by hand or on your bicycle. If you need to transport heavy items, consider renting a small cart or arranging delivery to one of the rest stops.

### 4. Check out agricultural stops and activities
Before you set off, consult the official event map (available on the organizers’ website) to identify the agricultural stops and activities that interest you. Some, like tastings, may require advance booking.

### 5. Plan your return trip
If you’re arriving by train, make sure you have your return ticket. If you’re cycling, check for secure bike parking at train stations, where you can safely leave your bicycle.

### Tools to help you organize
For those looking to plan their day in detail, the organizers’ website provides an interactive map of the route with all the stops and activities. You can also download the slowUp app to receive real-time updates on stops and any changes to the route.

📊 *Where to find the map and app*: [Official slowUp Ticino website](nav:transport)

### What to do if you’re a cross-border worker
If you work in Ticino but live in Lombardy, slowUp is a great opportunity to reduce car use and try alternative routes. For example, you could take the train to Locarno or Bellinzona and then explore the route on foot or by bike, perhaps combining the day with a visit to the historic centers or local markets.

💡 *Tip*: If you need to rent a bicycle, several local companies offer bike-sharing services for the event. Check availability and costs directly on the organizers’ website.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/riecco-slowup-ticino-vivi-una-giornata-senzauto-431488)*

item.title: Lombardy allocates 7 million euros for PhD researchers in SMEs: an opportunity for Ticino cross-border workers
item.description: The Lombardy Region is investing 7 million euros in the 'Talenti – Knowledge Transfer' call to hire PhD researchers in SMEs. Grants of up to 56,000 euros per year for 24 months are available for permanent employment contracts.
item.content:encoded:
The Lombardy Regional Government has approved the ‘Talenti – Trasferimento delle conoscenze’ call for proposals, a measure allocating **7 million euros** to encourage the hiring of PhD holders in small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) with operational headquarters in Lombardy. The declared aim is to create a stable bridge between laboratories and factories, accelerating the transfer of prototypes and innovative technologies to the market.

The measure, proposed by the Economic Development Counsellor Guido Guidesi and Research Counsellor Alessandro Fermi, seeks to retain talent within Lombardy and enhance its competitiveness. Fermi emphasised the need to provide qualified professional outlets beyond the academic sphere: *‘This is a strategic investment in human capital.’*

### Lombard SMEs and eligibility requirements
The call for proposals is open exclusively to SMEs based in Lombardy that intend to hire individuals with a PhD and have completed a collaboration with a research body within the past year. The grant cannot be combined with other regional incentives for the same position.

### Support: how the grant works
The regional support consists of a non-repayable contribution calculated on the gross annual cost of the hired personnel:

- Permanent employment: coverage of 70% of costs, with a maximum of 56,000 euros per year for up to 24 months.
- Fixed-term employment: coverage of 60% of costs, up to a maximum of 48,000 euros for a period not exceeding 12 months.

Each company may benefit from the grant for only one hire, which must be formalised after the official entry into force of the measure. Applications will be submitted exclusively online via the regional platform ‘Bandi e Servizi’, with operational procedures and deadlines to be announced shortly.

📊 Implications for Ticino cross-border workers
Cross-border workers residing in the Canton of Ticino who are interested in these positions may find an interesting professional opportunity. However, it is essential to verify contractual requirements and residency conditions, as the call for proposals is explicitly aimed at Lombard SMEs. Hires must be formalised after the measure enters into force, though the official date has not yet been specified but will be announced shortly.

> *‘Bringing advanced university training directly into the heart of production companies’* is the declared goal of the Lombardy Region. For cross-border workers, this could mean new career prospects in high-tech innovation sectors, with contracts featuring significant non-repayable contributions from the public entity. For cross-border workers from Ticino, the Lombardy tender represents a concrete opportunity, albeit with several **critical aspects to consider carefully**. The measure is designed for companies based in Lombardy, meaning that employees hired will primarily work on Italian soil, with potential implications for taxation and social security contributions.

### Comparing past and present: before and after the tender
Before this measure, small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in Lombardy already had access to tools for hiring qualified staff, though with lower contributions and stricter constraints. For example, previous regional tenders for PhD holders often covered less than 50% of costs or imposed lower spending limits. With this new measure, however, the 70% coverage for permanent hires marks a significant increase, particularly for SMEs looking to invest in innovation without excessively burdening payroll costs.

### Sectors most likely to benefit
Given the goal of knowledge transfer, the sectors that stand to gain the most from the tender include:

- Advanced technologies and Industry 4.0: automation, robotics, artificial intelligence.
- Biotech and life sciences: pharmaceuticals, medical devices, clinical research.
- Energy and sustainability: green tech, energy efficiency, circular economy.
- ICT and digitalisation: software, cybersecurity, cloud computing.

For cross-border workers, these fields offer fertile ground for high-value careers, especially when combined with language skills (Italian and German) or expertise in cross-cutting areas such as data management or regulatory compliance.

### Practical scenarios for cross-border workers
A Ticino cross-border worker interested in applying for one of these positions will need to weigh several factors:

1. Contract and workplace: The hiring is with an SME in Lombardy, so the primary workplace will be in Italy. However, some companies may offer partial remote working or trips to Ticino, particularly if they operate in cross-border sectors.

2. Taxation and social contributions: Income will be taxed in Italy, but workers will need to check how this affects their tax position in Ticino, especially in relation to the Italo-Swiss agreements to avoid double taxation.

3. Social security: Social security contributions will follow Italian regulations (INPS), but workers must ensure that their time working in Lombardy is recognised for pension purposes in Ticino, in line with the Italo-Swiss Convention of 9 December 1976.

4. Language and integration: While the tender is open to international candidates, proficiency in Italian is often an implicit requirement for working in Lombardy SMEs. For cross-border workers, this could be a competitive advantage, particularly if paired with German or English skills.

### Opportunities for Ticino SMEs
Although the tender is aimed at Lombardy SMEs, even Ticino-based companies operating in innovative sectors could indirectly benefit from this measure. For example, some SMEs in Ticino could forge partnerships with Lombardy SMEs for joint research projects, leveraging regional funding. In such cases, cross-border workers might take on transboundary coordination roles, with mixed contracts or secondment agreements.

### Risks and limitations
Despite the incentives, there are several limitations to consider:

- Duration of the benefit: The subsidy is valid for a maximum of 24 months for permanent hires. After this period, the company will have to bear the full cost of the employee.
- Single-use incentive: Each company can benefit from the tender only once, limiting hiring opportunities for firms looking to recruit multiple PhD holders.
- Competition: Lombardy SMEs may prefer candidates already residing in Lombardy to simplify contract and tax management, reducing opportunities for cross-border workers.

### How to prepare
Cross-border workers interested in applying should:

- Update their CV to highlight transferable skills (project management, digitalisation, compliance) and applied research experience.
- Verify their Italian language proficiency (at least B2 level) and, where possible, German.
- Monitor publications from Lombardy SMEs in their sectors of interest, often available on company websites or platforms like LinkedIn.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). To participate in the 'Talenti – Trasferimento delle conoscenze' call for proposals, Lombardy SMEs must follow a **strict procedure** involving several steps. Cross-border workers interested in applying for the open positions will need to prepare accordingly. Below is a step-by-step guide for both groups, with special attention to implications for those residing in Ticino.

### For Lombardy SMEs: How to submit an application

1. Verify requirements: Ensure your company is an SME with an operational headquarters in Lombardy and that the candidate holds a PhD with a completed research collaboration no more than one year prior with a research body.

2. Wait for the implementing notice: The operational procedures and deadlines for submitting applications have not yet been specified. The Lombardy Region will soon publish an official notice on the 'Bandi e Servizi' platform (bandi.regione.lombardia.it). It is essential to monitor this page to avoid missing deadlines.

3. Prepare documentation: SMEs will need to prepare the following:
 - Employment contract (permanent or fixed-term, depending on the choice).
 - Documentation proving the candidate’s PhD and collaboration with a research body.
 - An economic-financial plan demonstrating the project’s sustainability.

4. Online submission: Applications must be submitted exclusively via the regional 'Bandi e Servizi' platform, after registering. Paper applications or submissions through other channels will not be accepted.

5. Evaluation and grant approval: The Lombardy Region will evaluate applications based on criteria such as innovation, sustainability, and alignment with the call’s objectives. Evaluation timelines have not yet been specified, but the regional government has committed to announcing results as soon as possible.

### For Ticino cross-border workers: How to apply

1. Research open positions: Lombardy SMEs will publish job openings on:
 - Individual company websites (under 'Lavora con noi' or 'Work with us').
 - Platforms like LinkedIn, Indeed, or InfoJobs.
 - Regional portals such as Talent Lombardia (talent.regione.lombardia.it).

2. Prepare your CV and cover letter: Your CV should be concise and results-oriented, with particular emphasis on:
 - Experience in applied research or innovative projects.
 - Skills in digitalization, data management, and regulatory compliance (increasingly in demand).
 - Languages: Italian (B2/C1), German (useful for cross-border sectors), and English (required for many roles).

3. Submit your application: Send your CV and cover letter directly to the company, following the instructions provided in the job posting. Some SMEs may also require a video conference or in-person interview.

4. Contract verification: If you receive an offer, it is crucial to check the following:
 - Work location (may be in Italy, with possible business trips to Ticino).
 - Contract type: verify whether it is permanent or fixed-term, and the duration of the regional incentive.
 - Taxation: Income will be taxed in Italy, but you must verify how this impacts your tax return in Ticino (e.g., using the [cross-border worker tax calculator](nav:tax-return)).

5. Social security management: Ensure the company correctly applies Italian social security contributions (INPS). Upon returning to Ticino, verify with SUVA or INPS the recognition of the periods worked for pension purposes.

### Deadlines and timelines

> ⚠️ Important note: Deadlines and application procedures may change. Always refer to the official notices published on the Lombardy Region’s 'Bandi e Servizi' platform for the most up-to-date information.

> 📊 Key dates to remember:
> - Monitor the 'Bandi e Servizi' platform for the implementing notice.
> - Check company websites and job platforms regularly for new postings.
> - Prepare your application materials in advance to avoid last-minute delays.

> 💡 Tip for cross-border workers: Keep track of tax and social security implications, as these can vary depending on your contract and work location. Consulting a tax advisor or the relevant authorities in Ticino and Italy may be helpful.

[object Object]

💡 Final tip: This call for applications is a unique opportunity for cross-border workers from Ticino who want to work in high-innovation sectors. However, it is essential to carefully assess the contractual, tax and social security aspects before accepting an offer. Use the tools provided by Frontaliere Ticino to compare salaries, living costs and tax management, and make an informed decision.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/ricercatori-in-azienda-la-regione-stanzia-7-milioni-per-portare-i-talenti-nelle-pmi/2547118/)*

item.title: Chiasso shifts tiger mosquito control to private owners: what changes for cross-border workers
item.description: From May to October, the management of tiger mosquito hotspots in Chiasso will change. Private property owners will now be responsible for treating manholes on their land, while the municipality will provide free weekly biological larvicide treatments.
item.content:encoded:
The Municipality of Chiasso is revolutionizing its fight against the tiger mosquito with a new strategy set to take effect from May 2026. The key change involves the **management of breeding sites**: from now on, owners and managers of private properties will be responsible for drains, gully traps, and manholes on their land. Until last year, the municipal administration handled the entire municipal territory — both public and private — with targeted interventions every eight weeks.

This shift aligns with recommendations from SUPSI and other Ticinese regions that have already adopted this approach. The Municipality will continue to manage its own infrastructure, ensuring consistent public intervention. The frequency of interventions will increase to weekly, a crucial step to enhance the effectiveness of combating mosquito larvae.

### Why this decision?

The decision is driven by the need to more effectively curb the proliferation of the tiger mosquito, a vector for diseases such as dengue and chikungunya. According to SUPSI data, the population of *Aedes albopictus* is steadily rising in the Mendrisiotto region, with peaks recorded during the summer months. Weekly interventions at water stagnation points have proven decisive in reducing its spread, as evidenced by data from other Ticinese municipalities that have already adopted this practice.

The Municipality of Chiasso has therefore chosen to share responsibility for the mosquito control effort with citizens, equipping them with the tools needed to act effectively. The goal is to reduce the presence of the insect in the municipal area, thereby protecting public health and improving the city's livability.


> 📊 Key data: SUPSI reports a 20% increase in tiger mosquito populations in the Mendrisiotto region over the past three years.


### Affected infrastructure

The new strategy specifically targets:
- Private drains: accessible to property owners
- Gully traps and manholes: located on private properties
- Other stagnation points: such as uncovered water collection tanks or containers

The Municipality will instead manage:
- Drains and infrastructure owned by the public sector
- Public green spaces and parks
- Roads and sidewalks under municipal jurisdiction


### The role of private individuals

Owners of private properties and managers will receive detailed instructions on how to intervene, with particular emphasis on the use of biological larvicides provided free of charge by the Municipality. The product will be distributed upon request and accompanied by a technical sheet outlining its correct application. The municipal administration will also directly contact affected individuals to provide the necessary guidance.


> 💡 Tip: Regularly check gutters and containers for water accumulation, especially after rain.


### Timeline and deadlines

The tiger mosquito control campaign will run for six months, from May to October 2026. During this period, private individuals will be required to regularly monitor critical points on their properties and take prompt action in case of water stagnation. The Municipality will ensure weekly interventions on its own infrastructure, with cross-checks to verify compliance with the new regulations.


⚠️ Important: Failure to comply with the new rules may result in fines, as outlined in the municipal ordinance.


### Public health impact

The tiger mosquito poses a significant health risk, particularly during the summer when its activity peaks. According to SUPSI experts, the spread of this species is facilitated by environmental conditions increasingly similar to tropical climates, with mild winters and hot, humid summers. The Municipality of Chiasso has therefore decided to act promptly to prevent outbreaks of diseases transmitted by this insect, safeguarding residents and cross-border workers who transit through the area daily. For cross-border workers living or working in Chiasso, the new strategy against the tiger mosquito introduces some **practical changes** that could impact their daily routine. In particular, anyone owning a property, land, or commercial activity in the city will now need to take responsibility for managing mosquito breeding sites—a task previously handled exclusively by the municipality.

### What cross-border workers who own property must do

Cross-border commuters residing in Ticino but working in Lombardy who own a property in Chiasso—such as a holiday home or commercial premises—will now be required to:
- Monitor regularly the manholes, drainage grates, and inspection chambers on their land
- Apply the biological larvicide provided free of charge by the municipality, following the provided instructions
- Report promptly any water stagnation that cannot be managed independently

The municipality has set up a dedicated support service for property owners, with a dedicated contact point offering assistance by phone and email. Those concerned will also receive an official communication with detailed instructions on how to proceed.

### Key differences before and after

Previously, the municipality intervened on all manholes and stagnation points, both public and private, approximately every eight weeks. Now, responsibilities are divided:
- Private individuals: managing breeding sites on their own land
- Municipality: weekly interventions on public infrastructure and cross-checking

This shift introduces a shared responsibility model, requiring property owners to be more proactive in prevention. However, the municipality guarantees constant support, including the provision of larvicide and training.

### Concrete scenarios for cross-border workers

Scenario 1: Owner of a residential property in Chiasso
- Must apply larvicide every two months to the manholes in their garden
- Receives the product free of charge at the municipal offices
- Can request assistance with application

Scenario 2: Manager of a commercial premises in the city centre
- Must ensure external inspection chambers are not blocked
- Can request emergency intervention from the municipality in case of critical issues
- Must report any water accumulation in internal courtyards

Scenario 3: Cross-border worker renting an apartment
- Has no direct responsibility but can report issues to the property owner
- Can inform the municipality of unresolved critical situations

### Advantages and disadvantages

📊 Advantages of the new strategy:
- Greater effectiveness: weekly interventions drastically reduce breeding sites
- Lower costs for private individuals: larvicide is provided free of charge
- Increased awareness: citizens become key players in mosquito control

⚠️ Disadvantages:
- Increased commitment for property owners: constant management of their land is required
- Risk of penalties: in case of non-compliance, the municipality may intervene at the owner’s expense

### Comparison with other Ticinese municipalities

Chiasso is not the only municipality in the canton to adopt this strategy. Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno have also introduced similar systems, with positive results in reducing tiger mosquito populations. However, Chiasso stands out for its structured approach to managing private properties, with direct communication and ongoing support.

According to SUPSI data, municipalities that have adopted this strategy have recorded up to a 40% reduction in tiger mosquito breeding sites in urban areas. A figure that highlights the effectiveness of the new model, especially in densely populated areas like the Mendrisiotto region.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). The new strategy against the tiger mosquito in Chiasso requires **active involvement** from property owners and managers of private plots. Below is a step-by-step procedure to comply with obligations and benefit from municipal support.

### Step 1: Receiving the communication

By the end of April 2026, the Municipality of Chiasso will send an official communication to all property owners and managers of private plots, including the following details:
- Detailed instructions for managing breeding sites
- How to request biological larvicide
- Contact details for the municipal referent
- Deadlines and reporting procedures

If no communication is received by 30 April 2026, you must contact the Municipality directly to verify your status.

### Step 2: Requesting biological larvicide

The Municipality will provide larvicide free of charge to private individuals upon request. To obtain it, you must:
1. Fill out the online form available on the Municipality of Chiasso’s website
2. Or visit the municipal offices with a valid ID and proof of land ownership
3. Collect the product at the designated city centre

The larvicide is available in 1-litre containers, sufficient to treat around 20 drains. Each container comes with a technical data sheet and usage instructions.

### Step 3: Applying the larvicide

The product must be applied every two months during the tiger mosquito activity period (May–October). Here’s how to proceed:
1. Identify drains, gully grates, and inspection chambers on your property
2. Pour 5–10 ml of larvicide into each stagnation point, without exceeding the recommended dose
3. Record the application date in a personal log to track interventions
4. Report any issues or the need for extraordinary intervention to the Municipality

The product remains effective for 8 weeks, after which the process must be repeated.

### Step 4: Monitoring and reporting

During the summer season, it is essential to regularly monitor critical points. In case of:
- Water accumulation that cannot be managed independently
- Presence of larvae despite larvicide application
- Damage to infrastructure (broken drains, clogged chambers)

you must promptly report the situation to the Municipality via:
- Email: [address to be specified]
- Phone: [number to be specified]
- Online form: available on the municipal website

The Municipality will intervene within 48 hours to resolve the issue.

### Step 5: Inspections and penalties

The Municipality will conduct random checks to verify compliance with the new provisions. In case of non-compliance, the following measures will be taken:
1. Formal notice requesting regularisation within 15 days
2. Municipal intervention at the owner’s expense
3. Administrative fine for failure to apply larvicide

Fines vary depending on the severity of the non-compliance but can reach up to 500 CHF for repeated cases.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers

To facilitate compliance with obligations, the Municipality of Chiasso and SUPSI have prepared:
- An interactive map of critical points in Chiasso, available online
- A calendar of interventions with deadlines for larvicide application
- A telephone assistance service for clarifications and practical support

These tools are accessible via the official websites of the Municipality of Chiasso and SUPSI.

### What happens if obligations are not met?

Cross-border workers who fail to comply with the obligations may face both practical and financial consequences. For example:
- Risk of penalties: as mentioned earlier, the Municipality can intervene ex officio by charging the associated costs
- Tiger mosquito spread: the failure to apply the larvicide can promote the proliferation of the insect, with potential repercussions on public health
- Property issues: in case of rental agreements, the owner may be held liable for any damage caused by negligence

### Frequently asked questions

Who can request the larvicide?

All property owners and managers of private plots in Chiasso can request the larvicide. A document proving ownership or management of the land must be presented.

What happens if the land is rented?

In case of a rental agreement, the responsibility lies with the property owner. However, the tenant can report any issues to the owner and provide support in managing the situation.

Is the larvicide toxic to pets?

No, the larvicide provided by the Municipality is biological and non-toxic to pets or humans. However, direct contact with the product should be avoided, and it should be stored in a safe place.

What to do in case of absence during the summer season?

If you are unable to manage your land during the tiger mosquito season, you can delegate a representative or request an extraordinary intervention from the Municipality. It is essential to communicate your situation promptly.

### Useful resources

For further details and to download the necessary documents:
- [Municipality of Chiasso website](https://www.chiasso.ch)
- [SUPSI - Department of Environmental Sciences](https://www.supsi.ch/dsa)
- [Interactive map of critical areas](https://www.chiasso.ch/mappa-punti-critici)

### CTA: Calculate your cost of living in Chiasso

Tackling the tiger mosquito issue is just one of many expenses cross-border workers need to consider when moving to or working in Ticino. To get a clearer picture of the actual costs, we recommend using our [cost of living calculator](nav:cost-of-living) and comparing expenses between Chiasso and your city of residence in Italy.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/mendrisiotto/1917758/a-chiasso-la-lotta-contro-la-zanzara-tigre-si-fa-piu-intensa)*

item.title: Ticino and Campione d’Italia: the Joint Commission reviews services
item.description: Norman Gobbi chairs the annual meeting with Campione d’Italia. Ticino’s services, waste management and healthcare take centre stage in the discussion. Municipal elections on the horizon.
item.content:encoded:
Today, Campione d’Italia hosted the annual meeting of the Joint Commission between the Canton of Ticino and the Municipality of Campione d’Italia, chaired by Ticino State Councillor Norman Gobbi. The fourth such meeting since the enclave joined the European customs area on 1 January 2020, the discussions focused on three key dossiers: the provision of services by Ticino-based entities and companies, the collection and disposal of solid waste and inert materials, and socio-health services.

### A Commission with clear tasks
The Joint Commission, established by the Declaration on Cooperation between the Republic and Canton of Ticino and the Municipality of Campione d’Italia on 6 September 2011, serves as a permanent body for consultation, mediation, coordination, advance notice, and oversight between Ticino and Campione institutions. Its role has become even more critical following the amendment to the customs statute that brought Campione d’Italia into the European customs area, necessitating new agreements for the daily management of cross-border coexistence.

The discussion took place in a cordial and constructive atmosphere, as highlighted by the Ticino delegation. During the meeting, financial aspects and various files—both general and sector-specific—were also addressed, with particular attention to areas that have shown challenges since the regulatory changes. The Commission took the opportunity to review its mandate ahead of the upcoming municipal elections in Campione d’Italia and thanked the outgoing members of the Campione delegation.

### Key figures at the meeting
The Commission is chaired by Norman Gobbi, Ticino State Councillor, and includes Michele Foletti (Mayor of Lugano), Andrea Incerti (Mayor of Bissone), and, from Campione d’Italia, Roberto Canesi (Mayor), Cristina Ferrari, Emanuele Verda, and Enrico Lironi. The coordination is handled by Francesco Quattrini, Ticino Delegate for External Relations. Each figure represents a crucial link in the decision-making chain connecting the two territories.

### Hot topics: from waste to healthcare
The waste dossier addressed the collection and disposal of solid and inert materials, a matter directly impacting the lives of residents and cross-border workers who cross the border daily. Integrated management between Ticino and Campione requires precise agreements to avoid discrepancies or overlapping competences.

As for socio-health services, the focus was on ensuring continuity of care for citizens living or working in both territories. Collaboration between Ticino and Campione entities is essential to guarantee seamless access to healthcare and services, particularly amid growing cross-border mobility.

### Municipal elections: a pivotal moment
The proximity of the municipal elections in Campione d’Italia made the meeting an opportunity to assess the Commission’s work over the years and prepare for a new phase. The outgoing members of the Campione delegation were thanked for their contributions, while the new representatives will face challenges such as resource management and adapting services to the evolving needs of the territory.

> *The Joint Commission plays a vital role in ensuring smooth cooperation between Ticino and Campione d’Italia, especially after the enclave’s integration into the European customs area.* 📊 The joint Ticino-Campione d’Italia commission meeting is far from a mere formality—it’s a tangible step forward for those who live and work along the border. Decisions made here have immediate repercussions on the daily lives of thousands, from cross-border workers and entrepreneurs to residents shuttling between the two countries. Here’s what’s changing and what still needs to be defined.

## Pre- vs. post-2020: a shifting landscape
Before Campione d’Italia joined the EU customs area, service management between the two territories followed entirely different rules. Today, harmonising procedures and competencies is essential to prevent inefficiencies. For instance, solid urban waste collection was once handled separately, with conflicting regulations between Ticino and Campione. Now, the Commission must devise shared solutions to ensure compliance with EU standards while maintaining efficiency.

The daily commute of cross-border workers into Ticino represents a significant portion of the population. For them, the continuity of socio-health services is critical: consider those who rely on medical care or access to Ticino’s healthcare facilities despite living in Campione. The Commission must guarantee these services remain accessible and coordinated, avoiding redundancies or gaps.

📊 **Key challenges ahead**:
- Harmonising waste collection protocols
- Ensuring seamless healthcare access for cross-border residents
- Preventing bureaucratic hurdles in service provision

### Ticino’s services: a lifeline for Campione d’Italia
Ticino’s public entities and companies provide services that have become indispensable for Campione. This includes transport, healthcare, education, and waste management. The Commission’s role is to monitor service quality and ensure these offerings remain efficient for Campione’s residents. A concrete example? The disposal of inert waste—an operation requiring tight coordination to prevent non-compliant disposal.

### Socio-healthcare services: a delicate balance
Socio-healthcare is one of the most complex dossiers. Residents of Campione who work in Ticino—or vice versa—must access care and services without barriers. The Commission must verify that existing agreements are upheld and address any issues promptly. A simple billing error in healthcare services, for instance, could trigger bureaucratic headaches for patients.

### What happens if no agreement is reached?
🚨 Risks are real:
- Delays in waste management, with potential environmental and health consequences
- Overlapping costs for citizens, who may end up paying multiple times for the same service

The Commission’s role is to act as a safeguard against these scenarios.

### Mid-term assessment
The Commission is compiling an end-of-term report, set to be presented before Campione’s municipal elections. This document will evaluate progress made and highlight areas needing further intervention. Among the strengths? The constructive dialogue between parties. Among the challenges? The timeframes for regulatory adjustments and the need for additional resources in certain sectors.

💡 Looking ahead:
The next steps will focus on refining coordination mechanisms and securing the necessary funding to sustain seamless cross-border services.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers and residents of Campione d’Italia who live straddling the two territories, the decisions made by the Paritetica Commission are not just bureaucratic matters—they directly impact daily life. Here’s what you need to do to verify your rights, how to act in case of service disruptions, and useful tools to navigate the cross-border landscape.

### How to check your status
1. Healthcare services: If you are a cross-border worker residing in Campione but working in Ticino, ensure your Swiss health insurance is up to date. If in doubt, consult your health insurance provider or use the [socio-health benefits calculator](nav:health).
2. Waste collection: If you live in Campione but use Ticino’s waste disposal services, check with your local municipality for the latest procedures. The Commission has emphasised the need to harmonise these services, though practical details may vary.
3. Travel documents: Since 2020, crossing the border requires extra attention. Always carry a valid ID and, if necessary, documents proving your employment in Ticino.

### What to do in case of service disruptions
If you encounter issues with waste management, healthcare access, or other areas discussed by the Commission, the first step is to contact your local municipality. In Campione, Mayor Roberto Canesi or his team can direct you to the relevant authorities. In Ticino, you can reach out to the competent cantonal offices, such as the [Department of Finance and Economy](nav:companies).

### Deadlines and procedures to keep in mind
- Municipal elections in Campione: The next elections are approaching. If you are a resident voter in Campione, verify your registration on the electoral rolls. For doubts, consult the Campione d’Italia municipal website.
- Regulatory updates: The Commission is working on new agreements. Keep an eye on official communications from the Canton of Ticino and the Municipality of Campione d’Italia to stay informed.
- **Healthcare services**: If you need medical assistance in Ticino but reside in Campione, request prior authorisation from your Swiss health insurance provider. The [cross-border healthcare services guide](nav:health) can help you navigate the system.

📊 Tools for cross-border workers
Managing taxes and social security contributions is often a source of confusion for cross-border workers. The [cross-border salary calculator](nav:payslip) can help you verify applicable rates and your entitlements. If you’re looking for work in Ticino, consult the [municipal map](nav:border-map) to identify areas with higher labour demand.

💡 A practical case: managing inert waste
Imagine living in Campione but needing to dispose of inert materials generated by your work in Ticino. The Commission has highlighted the need for a specific agreement to prevent these materials from being disposed of improperly. If you encounter issues, contact your local municipality for clarification on updated procedures.

⚠️ Useful contacts
- Municipality of Campione d’Italia: [Official website](https://www.comune.campione-d-italia.va.it)
- Canton of Ticino – Cross-border services: [DFE Ticino](https://www.ti.ch/dfe)
- Health insurance: Contact your provider to verify coverage for cross-border healthcare services.

### Practical conclusions
The decisions made by the Paritetica Commission are not merely theoretical—they have a tangible impact on the lives of those who live and work between Ticino and Campione. Whether you’re a cross-border worker, an entrepreneur, or a resident, stay updated on developments and don’t hesitate to seek clarification from the competent authorities. Collaboration between the two territories is the only way to ensure efficient, seamless services.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/il-ticino-incontra-campione-ditalia-431384)*

item.title: Ticino nurses: fleeing to Milan in search of work
item.description: A recent SUPSI graduate speaks out about the lack of opportunities in Ticino. While 94.9% of SUPSI graduates find employment, Martina’s case reveals a far grimmer reality.
item.content:encoded:
“It’s frustrating not being able to put into practice, in a professional setting, the skills I’ve learned over years of training.” These are the words of Martina*, 26, a newly qualified nurse with a Master of Science in Nursing from the University of Applied Sciences and Arts of Southern Switzerland (SUPSI). She began her job search in May 2025, but after nearly a year without concrete results, the young nurse decided to look beyond Switzerland’s borders. “I sent my CV to the European Institute of Oncology (IEO) in Milan. It’s a very specific field, and I didn’t expect my application to be considered. Yet, the day after I submitted it, I was contacted and invited for an interview. Since last May, they’ve been trying to reach me.”

Martina’s story comes to light in a context where, according to SUPSI data, 94.9% of graduates in 2023 had found employment within a year of earning their degree. However, Professor Carla Pedrazzani, head of the SUPSI Bachelor’s programme in Nursing, points out that “patient care pathways are changing: we’re seeing a clear shift from acute care to long-term and community-based care.” This shift in training doesn’t always align with the expectations of new graduates, who often seek opportunities in hospitals.

Martina worked for a few months in a doctor’s office, but when her contract expired, it wasn’t renewed. “I don’t know if it’s a question of credibility, but there’s no real opportunity to engage with the profession. I feel discouraged: on one hand, experience is demanded, on the other, there’s no chance to gain it. It’s a Catch-22.” Her experience isn’t unique. Some colleagues in similar situations have temporarily set aside their hospital ambitions to turn to nursing homes or other sectors, but even in those fields, the situation hasn’t proven any easier.

> ## The contradiction between data and reality
>
> SUPSI’s 2024 figures show that 94.9% of 2023 graduates found employment within a year of earning their degree. Yet Professor Pedrazzani admits that “every single case is different.” The need for nurses in Ticino has become chronic in recent years, with repeated warnings about professionals leaving the field. But what happens to those, like Martina, who can’t secure a position in the local job market?

The answer isn’t obvious. According to SUPSI, training is adapting to a demand that is shifting from acute care to community-based and long-term care. However, for many new graduates, the hospital remains the ultimate goal. “Working in a doctor’s office is a reality where you don’t truly engage with nursing duties: it might be fine for a while, but not in the long run. Even in care homes for the elderly, the story is much the same. Everyone I know can’t wait to move on,” explains Martina.

### The scale of the phenomenon

SUPSI’s 2024 data shows an employment rate of 94.9% for 2023 graduates, but it doesn’t specify how many found jobs in hospitals compared to other facilities. Professor Pedrazzani highlights that “there’s a perceived reluctance in some sectors to trust new graduates.” This phenomenon could explain why, despite the demand for staff, some young professionals struggle to secure stable positions.

### What changes for cross-border workers

For those living in Ticino but looking across the border, the situation becomes even more complex. Martina decided to send her CV to Milan after months of unsuccessful job searches. “It’s a leap of faith, but when you’re given no alternatives, you have to try.” Her experience raises questions about professional mobility and the opportunities the Italian job market can offer compared to the Swiss one.

📊 **Key figures**
- 94.9% of SUPSI nursing graduates in 2023 found employment within a year.
- Demand for nurses in Ticino has been chronic for years, with repeated warnings about staff shortages.
- Many new graduates struggle to gain experience due to the “experience paradox.”

💡 Insight
The shift from acute to community-based care is reshaping training needs, but hospitals remain the preferred destination for many. This mismatch may contribute to the difficulties faced by new graduates in securing stable positions.

⚠️ Call to action
The healthcare sector must address the experience paradox by creating structured pathways for new graduates to gain practical experience, ensuring a sustainable workforce for the future. Martina’s story is not just a personal case, but reflects a broader phenomenon affecting young nurses in Ticino. Despite the chronic need for healthcare staff, many recent graduates struggle to find employment in their field of specialization. This imbalance between labor supply and demand is not new, but recent dynamics are highlighting shortcomings that have never been so evident.

### Before and after: the changing expectations

Until just a few years ago, the typical career path for a nurse in Ticino involved securing a position in a hospital within months of graduating. Today, however, many young professionals are forced to accept fixed-term contracts or roles in sectors considered "fallback" options, such as medical practices or nursing homes. "I sent my resume to every facility, but apart from a few contacts, nothing materialized," Martina explains. Her testimony highlights a growing gap between educational expectations and the realities of the job market, especially for those aspiring to work in hospitals.

### The problem of lack of experience

One of the main barriers for recent graduates is employers’ demand for prior work experience. "On one hand, they ask for experience, but on the other, they don’t give you the chance to gain it. It’s a paradox," says the 26-year-old. While this issue is not new, its persistence points to a structural problem in Ticino’s labor market. Despite needing staff, healthcare facilities appear to favor candidates with at least a few years of experience, leaving recent graduates at a disadvantage.

### Alternatives: the border and mobility

For many young professionals, the solution lies beyond the border. Martina decided to send her resume to the European Institute of Oncology (IEO) in Milan, a specialized center that responded positively to her application. "It’s a very specific field, and I didn’t expect my application to even be considered. Yet, the day after sending my CV, I was contacted and invited for an interview." Her experience shows that, in some cases, the Italian market can offer opportunities that Ticino’s labor market fails to provide.

However, working abroad also comes with additional challenges, such as the need to obtain new professional certification or adapt to a different healthcare system. "I don’t know if it’s a credibility issue, but you’re not given the chance to truly engage with the profession," Martina reflects. Her frustration is shared by many colleagues who, despite completing high-level training, find themselves forced to seek opportunities elsewhere.

### The role of educational institutions

SUPSI is working to align its training programs with the evolving needs of the job market, with a focus on community care and long-term care. "We’re trying to adapt our training to this shift and present all professional perspectives to our students," explains Professor Pedrazzani. However, the transition is not immediate, and many recent graduates feel disoriented by a job market that no longer matches their expectations.

### What if...?

If the trend of nurses leaving Ticino for jobs abroad were to grow, two opposing scenarios could unfold:

- **Scenario 1:** Ticino’s healthcare facilities may be forced to revise their hiring policies, offering more opportunities to recent graduates to prevent the loss of talent.
- Scenario 2: The shortage of qualified staff in Ticino could push more young professionals to seek work abroad, further reducing the local pool of available talent.

In both cases, the risk is that Ticino’s healthcare system will lose valuable expertise, with long-term consequences for the quality of care provided.

> 📊 Key takeaway: The mismatch between training and job market realities is pushing young nurses to look beyond Ticino’s borders.

> ⚠️ Food for thought: If no action is taken, Ticino’s healthcare system may face a brain drain, with serious implications for patient care.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). Are you a Ticino-based nurse looking for job opportunities? Your experience might be similar to Martina’s. Here’s how you can maximize your chances of finding employment, both in Ticino and across the border.

### Step 1: Assess local opportunities

Before looking beyond the border, it’s crucial to explore all possibilities in the Ticino job market. SUPSI offers career coaching and placement services for its graduates, helping them identify healthcare facilities interested in newly qualified professionals. «Presenting all professional perspectives to our students» is one of SUPSI’s priorities, as confirmed by Professor Pedrazzani.

You can also directly contact Ticino healthcare facilities, such as EOC (Ente Ospedaliero Cantonale) hospitals, to ask about graduate integration programs. Some hospitals offer training pathways that allow you to gain hands-on experience.

### Step 2: Expand your search across the border 📊

If opportunities in Ticino are limited, consider applying to healthcare facilities in Italy, particularly in specialized fields like oncology. Martina found an opportunity at the European Institute of Oncology (IEO) in Milan, but there are also other facilities in Lombardy and Piedmont actively seeking nursing staff.

However, working in Italy requires obtaining the Italian professional qualification. The process varies by region, but generally includes:

- Submitting a request for title recognition to the *Ordine delle Professioni Infermieristiche* (OPI) of the destination region.
- Completing any required additional training.
- Passing a state exam or an evaluative internship.

Title recognition is governed by the **EU Directive 2005/36/CE**, which simplifies professional mobility between EU countries. However, the process can take several months, so it’s advisable to start the procedure well in advance.

### Step 3: Prepare an effective CV 💡

A well-structured CV is essential to catch the attention of recruiters. Here are some practical tips:

-Tailor your CV** to the job offer, highlighting the most relevant skills for the role.
- Include training experiences such as internships or university projects that demonstrate your abilities.
- Add references from professors or professionals in the field who can attest to your skills.

If you’re looking for a job in Italy, make sure your CV is in Italian and follows local conventions. Some facilities may also require a motivational cover letter.

### Step 4: Use the resources available

SUPSI offers job search support services, including:

- [Career coaching](nav:jobs) to help you prepare your CV and handle interviews.
- [Placement platforms](nav:jobs) to find job announcements specifically for nurses.
- [Assistance for title recognition](nav:permits) if you wish to work abroad.

In addition to these services, you can register on specialized healthcare job portals, such as:

- [JobUp](https://www.jobup.ch) for announcements in Switzerland.
- [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com) for international opportunities.
- [Indeed](https://www.indeed.it) for announcements in Italy.

### Step 5: Evaluate the possibility of working as a frontier employee ⚠️

If you decide to work in Italy but reside in Ticino, you’ll need to consider tax and insurance aspects. As a frontier employee, you’re entitled to specific tax benefits, but you must also comply with certain rules.

You can calculate your net salary and taxes owed using our [salary calculator](nav:payslip). Additionally, ensure you’re compliant with healthcare and social security insurance, both in Switzerland and Italy.


### What to do if you can’t find a job?

If, after months of searching, you still haven’t found employment, consider:

- Attending specialization courses to enhance your skills and become more competitive.
- Offering your services as a volunteer in healthcare facilities to gain practical experience.
- Participating in job fairs, such as the one organized by SUPSI, to meet recruiters directly.

Your training is an added value: don’t give up, and keep searching for your opportunity.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1913967/controtendenza-frustrazione-milano-ospedale-martina)*

item.title: Bibo: the digital ticket that activates automatically on Ticino’s public transport
item.description: Starting 30 April 2026, Switzerland will launch a trial of Bibo, which automatically bills journeys without requiring passengers to check in. Here’s how it works for Ticino commuters
item.content:encoded:
From April 30, 2026, around 3,000 users in Switzerland will test the **Bibo** system (*Be in - be out*) for two months—a new feature that eliminates the need to manually purchase or validate tickets on public transport. The initiative, developed by Switzerland’s transport sector and promoted by Alliance Swisspass, will enter a pilot phase just as Ticino prepares to collect data that could pave the way for extending the application beyond the national border.

### How Bibo works in practice
The system relies on Bluetooth technology: all users need is a phone with Bluetooth enabled and the Swisspass app installed. The app automatically detects when passengers board or alight from trains, buses, or trams in Italian-speaking Switzerland—no screen interaction required. '*The system calculates the cost of the journeys taken and invoices the user in the evening*,' explains Michaela Ruoss from Alliance Swisspass. It follows a similar logic to the existing EasyRide, but with one key difference: no more check-in or check-out is required, an action often forgotten by distracted commuters, which can lead to fines.

### Immediate benefits for cross-border workers
For those who cross the border daily between Varese, Como, and Ticino, Bibo means saving time and bureaucracy. Until now, Ticino commuters have had to rely on:
- purchasing monthly or annual passes;
- validating tickets at station machines upon entry;
- periodically checking their routes on portals like [trasporti.ch](nav:transport).

With Bibo, travel becomes completely automatic: the cost is calculated based on the distance traveled and the time of day, much like contactless ticketing systems already in use in some Italian cities. '*This is a step forward for the digitalization of public transport, which could also reduce queues at ticket offices in Chiasso, Mendrisio, and Bellinzona*,' comments an SBB (Swiss Federal Railways) official interviewed by RSI.

### Timeline for rollout
The official test will begin on April 30, 2026, and last for two months. Only afterward will it be assessed whether to integrate Bibo into the official SBB app, which already manages passes for cross-border commuters. For now, participation is limited to a selected group of users, but the initiative could soon involve Ticino residents who travel between Como and Varese.

### Privacy concerns: the critical issue
As highlighted by the Pro Bahn association, the system must fully comply with Swiss data protection regulations. '*It is essential that each passenger’s route data remains confidential and is not used for any other purposes*,' states the association’s spokesperson. SBB has already confirmed that data will be processed in accordance with current regulations, but the system’s resilience against unauthorized access remains to be verified.

### What changes compared to EasyRide
📊
| Feature | EasyRide | Bibo |
|---------|----------|------|
| Ticket activation | Manual (check-in/check-out) | Automatic (Bluetooth) |
| Risk of fines | High (if check-out is forgotten) | Zero (ticket always valid) |
| Cost | Linked to pass or single trip | Calculated based on distance traveled |
| Availability | Already operational in some cities | In testing until June 30, 2026 |

Before Bibo, Ticino commuters had to remember to validate their tickets within 30 seconds of boarding. A mistake could result in fines of up to 100 CHF, according to SBB tariffs. Now, with the new system, that risk disappears—but questions remain about the reliability of Bluetooth technology in tunnels or areas with weak signals, such as the stretch between Stabio and Varese.

> ⚠️ Note: The Bibo system is designed to operate seamlessly across borders, but its effectiveness in cross-border areas will depend on the stability of Bluetooth connections in transit zones. ### Bibo for cross-border workers: opportunities and challenges in daily border crossings

The introduction of Bibo could revolutionise the routines of over **70,000 cross-border workers** who cross the border between Italy and Ticino every day, particularly for those using the S10 (Varese-Como-Lugano), S30 (Bellinzona-Lugano) train lines and TiLo bus services. But what are the real advantages and potential pitfalls of this technology?

📊 A practical scenario: from Como to Lugano without a second thought

Imagine taking the morning train from Como Nord at 7:45 AM, heading to Lugano. With Bibo, you no longer have to:
- Buy a ticket at the Chiasso machine;
- Remember to validate it before departure;
- Report your arrival in Mendrisio or Lugano;
- Worry about validation errors.

The system automatically records the start of your journey when your phone detects the Bluetooth signal from the departure station and concludes it when you exit the train in Lugano. The cost will be charged directly to your Swisspass account or linked credit card, with no manual intervention required. *"For commuters accustomed to flexible schedules or last-minute trips, this means less stress and smoother daily commutes,"* explains an employee at a Ticino-based company that has already tested a beta version of the software.

⚠️ The risks: what happens if your phone runs out of battery?

One of Bibo’s key weaknesses is battery life. If your phone dies during the journey, the system may fail to record the route correctly, leaving you at risk of either not being charged or, in the worst case, facing a fine for an unpaid journey. To mitigate this risk, Alliance Swisspass recommends:
- Keeping your phone fully charged;
- Enabling airplane mode only after leaving the vehicle;
- Having a backup plan, such as purchasing an emergency ticket via the Swisspass app.

Another critical issue is signal coverage on less-served routes, such as the Valmorea-Stabio railway line. Here, Bluetooth reception may be intermittent, forcing commuters to revert to the traditional system. *"In these areas, Bibo’s testing will need to prove it is as reliable as a paper ticket,"* comments a representative from SEV (Ticino Transport Union).

💡 Bibo vs. traditional season tickets: which is more cost-effective?

For a commuter travelling 50 km daily between Varese and Lugano (around 30,000 km per year), the current cost with a monthly season ticket is around 200 CHF, while with Bibo, the price would be calculated based on the actual distance travelled. According to preliminary estimates from Alliance Swisspass, a round trip could cost between 8 and 12 CHF, depending on the time of day.

However, for regular travellers, a season ticket may still be more economical. Here’s a quick comparison:

[Compare Bibo and season ticket options](nav:compare_tickets)

*Costs are approximate and subject to change based on Alliance Swisspass updates.*

## Cost comparison: Bibo vs. traditional tickets

| User type | Current cost (subscription) | Estimated cost with Bibo (daily) |
|----------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Varese-Lugano commuters | 200 CHF/month | 10-15 CHF/day (200-300 CHF/month) |
| Occasional user | 50 CHF/day pass | 5-10 CHF/day |
| SUPSI/USI students | 120 CHF/semester | 3-8 CHF/day |

As shown, daily commuters may not necessarily save money with Bibo, but the system simplifies their travel. For occasional users, however, Bibo could offer a more flexible alternative.

### Impact on local traffic
💡 Greater digitalization of public transport could reduce queues at Ticino’s train stations, such as Chiasso, Mendrisio, and Lugano. Currently, during peak hours, waits of up to 10 minutes are recorded for single-trip tickets. With Bibo, this time could be reinvested by users in other activities, such as breakfast or preparing for work.

### Associations’ feedback: positive, with reservations
Pro Bahn has expressed a positive view of Bibo, provided that current ticket-purchasing options remain available. '*It’s important that those who still prefer paper tickets or traditional subscriptions are not disadvantaged*,' states a spokesperson. The Swiss Federal Railways (FFS), for their part, see the system as an opportunity to boost customer satisfaction, particularly among young people and Italian commuters who often struggle to navigate Swiss fare structures.

### Next steps: what to monitor after the trial
Over the next two months, Alliance Swisspass will collect data on:
- System reliability under various conditions (e.g., tunnels, crowded stations);
- User satisfaction via questionnaires;
- Impact on fare evasion fines (expected to drop to zero with Bibo).

Only after the trial is complete will the FFS Board of Directors decide whether to roll out Bibo to all users. '*If the system proves robust, it could be integrated as early as 2027*,' an FFS executive told RSI.

### A transport revolution, but not for everyone
📊 Bibo marks progress in digitalization, but it won’t solve all commuters’ problems. For example, those who cross the border by car or motorcycle will still need to rely on:
- A vignette for Swiss highways;
- Vehicle registration;
- Third-party liability insurance.

For them, the changes will be limited to train or bus journeys. However, for those who travel daily between Italy and Ticino, Bibo could be the definitive solution to bid farewell to queues and fines.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### How to activate Bibo: step-by-step guide for commuters

If you are among the 3,000 selected for the Bibo test, here’s how to proceed to activate the system and start travelling stress-free. This procedure also applies to cross-border commuters who cross the border between Varese, Como and Ticino every day.

📊 **Minimum requirements**
Before you begin, make sure you have:
1. A compatible smartphone (iOS 15+ or Android 10+);
2. The Swisspass app installed and updated to the latest version;
3. Bluetooth enabled on your phone;
4. A credit card or a Swisspass account linked for payments;
5. A free Alliance Swisspass account.

If you don’t yet have the Swisspass app, you can download it for free from the Apple or Google Play store. Make sure to register with your personal details (name, surname, address), as the system will link your journeys to your profile.

### Activating the Bibo system
1. Turn on Bluetooth on your phone (this is essential for automatic detection).
2. Open the Swisspass app and select the ‘*Activate Bibo*’ option in the main menu.
3. Accept the terms of use and the privacy policy. The system will ask for permission to track your location during your journey.
4. Link a payment method: you can choose between a credit card, a Swiss bank account or your Swisspass balance.
5. Test the system with a short journey (for example, the Mendrisio-Lugano route) to ensure everything is working correctly.

> «*If your phone does not detect boarding, try restarting the app or resetting your Bluetooth settings. If problems persist, you can always purchase an emergency ticket via the app.*» — Alliance Swisspass technical note

### Travelling with Bibo: what to do and what to avoid
Once Bibo is activated, the system will work automatically. Here are some tips to avoid surprises:
- Do not turn off your phone during the journey: you risk not recording the route.
- Do not put your phone in airplane mode before alighting: the system needs to detect your exit from the vehicle.
- If your journey is interrupted (for example, a train change), the system will automatically record the complete route.
- If you arrive at your destination without your phone having recorded the route, you can report it via the Swisspass app within 24 hours. The support team will assess the case.

💡 Managing errors and assistance
If you encounter any issues, you can contact the Swisspass customer service via:
- In-app chat in the Swisspass app (available 24/7);
- Phone: +41 848 848 844 (free from Swiss landlines);
- Email: support@swisspass.ch.

Always provide the following details:
- Your Swisspass customer number;
- The date and time of the problematic journey;
- A brief description of the error (for example, ‘the phone turned off during the journey’).

⚠️ Deadlines and what to expect after June 2026
The Bibo test will end on 30 June 2026. By 15 July 2026, Alliance Swisspass will publish a report with the results and any decisions on extending the system.

If the test is successful, Bibo could be integrated into the official SBB app as early as 2027, with a phased rollout that will first involve Ticino commuters and then all of Canton Ticino.

### What if Bibo isn’t extended?
Even if the project fails, commuters won’t be left without alternatives. The following options will remain available:
- Monthly/annual passes that can be purchased online or at ticket counters;
- Single tickets via the Swisspass app;
- Discounts for cross-border commuters as provided by the Italo-Swiss agreements (e.g., reductions on annual passes).

### Tips for cross-border commuters not taking part in the test
If you’re not among the 3,000 selected users, you can still prepare for Bibo:

1. Download the Swisspass app and create an account;
2. Enable Bluetooth every time you take a train or bus;
3. Keep an eye on official announcements from Alliance Swisspass, which will publish updates on the project’s future.

💡 Save on transport beyond Bibo
While waiting for Bibo’s evolution, you can already optimise your journeys with these tools:
- [Cost of living calculator for cross-border commuters](nav:cost-of-living) to compare expenses on transport and accommodation;
- [Health insurance comparator](nav:health) to save on insurance costs linked to your commute;
- [Guide to SBB passes for cross-border commuters](nav:border-map) to find the most cost-effective option.

All that’s left to do is wait for the test results and prepare for a future where Ticino public transport tickets will be purchased automatically.
